questions.specification: Which of the following describes an environmental consideration specific to the 'utilization' stage of a product's Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)?

options.content: The energy efficiency of an appliance while in operation by the consumer
The quantity of packaging materials required for safe transit
The recyclability of the materials used in the chassis
The carbon emissions from the factory where the product is assembled

questions.specification: A company is developing a new line of eco-friendly furniture. The furniture needs to be both functional and sustainable. The design team must consider various properties of materials and their impact on performance and the environment.

parts.content: Identify two sustainable materials commonly used in eco-friendly furniture and discuss their suitability.
parts.markscheme:
- Identification of bamboo as a sustainable material <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation of bamboo's suitability:
  - Fast-growing renewable resource
  - No pesticides required
  - Strong and durable with high tensile strength <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identification of reclaimed wood as a sustainable material <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation of reclaimed wood's suitability:
  - Reduces need for new lumber
  - Minimizes deforestation
  - Provides unique aesthetic
  - Durable properties <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for either characteristic or suitability explanation for each material</note>

parts.content: Explain the importance of using non-toxic finishes in eco-friendly furniture.
parts.markscheme:
- Non-toxic finishes reduce indoor air pollution and harmful VOC emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Made from natural ingredients and free from harmful chemicals, creating safer indoor environments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmentally beneficial as they minimize air/water pollution during production and application <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Make sure to link each point to both environmental and health benefits for full marks</examTip>

parts.content: Compare the environmental impact of using virgin materials versus recycled materials in furniture production.
parts.markscheme:
- Using virgin materials involves extraction of raw resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental impacts of virgin materials include:
  - Deforestation
  - Habitat destruction 
  - High energy consumption
  - Significant greenhouse gas emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Benefits of using recycled materials:
  - Reduces demand for new raw resources
  - Conserves energy
  - Minimizes waste <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recycled materials are more sustainable because:
  - Require less processing
  - Lower environmental impact
  - Contribute to circular economy <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Commercial manufacturers are increasingly adopting waste mitigation strategies to comply with environmental regulations and improve sustainability. A typical example is the high-volume modular office photocopier shown in **Figure 1**.

**Figure 1: A modular commercial photocopier**

![photocopier](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-63c6735e-c260-47b7-8534-9c74e74b0973-1771937587718/e6513426-48a3-4747-b49b-2bf978734724.jpeg)

parts.content: Outline how the shift from physical media distribution to digital cloud services (such as providing software manuals online) is an example of dematerialization.
parts.markscheme:
- Dematerialization involves reducing the total material and energy throughput of a product or service <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Moving manuals from paper to digital format eliminates the need for physical materials (paper, ink, packaging) and the energy required for their production/transport <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for the definition/explanation of dematerialization and 1 mark for the specific application to the digital shift.</note>

parts.content: Discuss whether the implementation of a manufacturer "take-back" scheme for used photocopiers is an example of **product recovery** or **pollution reduction**.
parts.markscheme:
- It is product recovery because the manufacturer collects the end-of-life product to extract value by salvaging functional parts or materials for reuse <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It is pollution reduction because it ensures hazardous components (like toner chemicals or lead-soldered boards) are disposed of through controlled channels rather than landfill <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It is generally considered both, as recovering materials automatically reduces the environmental pollution associated with virgin material extraction and waste disposal <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>
Maximum 1 mark for each point:
- Identifying product recovery aspect
- Identifying pollution reduction aspect
- Providing a concluding/integrating statement
</note>

parts.content:
A circular economy aims to keep resources in use for as long as possible.

Explain how waste can be minimized through **reconditioning**, **re-engineering** and **recycling** strategies in the management of commercial photocopiers.
parts.markscheme:
### Reconditioning <marks>3 marks max</marks>
- Reconditioning involves returning a used product to its original "as-new" functional and aesthetic state <marks>1 mark</marks>
- For a photocopier, this involves cleaning, repairing, and replacing worn internal rollers or belts while keeping the original chassis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Minimizes waste by extending the primary life of the machine, delaying the need for a new replacement <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Re-engineering <marks>3 marks max</marks>
- Re-engineering involves redesigning a product or its components to improve performance or update its functionality <marks>1 mark</marks>
- For a photocopier, this could involve replacing an obsolete analog processing unit with modern digital circuit boards while retaining the mechanical frame <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Minimizes waste by preventing the disposal of large mechanical structures that are still functional, even if the technology inside is outdated <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Recycling <marks>3 marks max</marks>
- Recycling is the process of breaking down a product into its base materials to be reprocessed into new products <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Components like the metal frame or plastic panels are shredded and melted down to create raw material for different products <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Minimizes waste by diverting materials from landfill and reducing the demand for virgin raw material extraction <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>
- Do not award marks across clusters (e.g., mixing reconditioning and re-engineering points).
- Mark as [3] + [3] + [3].
</note>

questions.specification:
A design team is applying eco-design principles to a new electric kettle to reduce its environmental impact. They decide to incorporate an automatic shut-off sensor that prevents the device from continuing to boil once the water has reached the target temperature.

At which stage of the Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) will this design feature provide the most significant reduction in resource consumption?

options.markscheme: Pre-production focuses on the extraction and sourcing of raw materials. While the sensor is a component made of materials, its primary environmental benefit is not related to the sourcing stage.

The 'Use' stage of a Life Cycle Analysis involves the environmental impacts generated during the product's functional life. An automatic shut-off sensor directly reduces energy consumption during operation by preventing wasted electricity after the boiling point is reached.
Distribution focuses on the packaging and transport of the product to the consumer. The addition of a sensor for boiling control does not significantly impact shipping or packaging efficiency.

Disposal (or End-of-Life) focuses on how the product is recycled, incinerated, or sent to a landfill. A functional sensor for energy efficiency during operation does not primarily target the waste management phase.

questions.specification: A design team is using the Eco-design Strategy (LiDS) wheel to evaluate potential sustainability improvements for a new household appliance. Which of the following correctly pairs a design action with its corresponding axis on the wheel?

options.content: Action: Replacing virgin plastics with recycled ocean plastic; Axis: Selection of low-impact materials
Action: Using high-durability components to prolong product use; Axis: Optimization of production techniques
Action: Reducing the number of different polymers to simplify sorting; Axis: Optimization of the distribution system
Action: Shifting from air freight to rail transport for bulk delivery; Axis: Optimization of initial lifetime

questions.specification: A food packaging company decides to stop using a specific chemical additive in their plastic containers because new, but inconclusive, research suggests it might be harmful to human health. Even though the chemical is currently legal, the company prioritizes environmental and consumer safety. Which concept of eco-design is being applied in this scenario?

A textile manufacturer develops a synthetic polyester fabric that can be chemically disassembled into its base monomers at the end of its lifecycle, allowing for the creation of new high-quality fibers indefinitely. Within the Cradle to Cradle (C2C) design framework, this material is classified as a:

A furniture manufacturer decides to switch from using virgin aluminium to $100\%$ recycled aluminium for a chair frame. This change is intended to reduce the total energy consumed during the extraction and processing of raw materials. In which stage of the Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) would this specific environmental benefit be categorized?

The following pie chart shows the results of a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) for a typical household refrigerator.

**Figure 1: Life cycle assessment distribution**
![LCA pie chart](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-bff39bee-d1cf-4fda-a45e-ee6ace1225e0-1771929956947/e917f07b-7976-4d3d-96f3-8828b23a0cfe.jpeg)

According to the data provided, which phase of the product life cycle represents the most significant opportunity for an eco-designer to reduce the product's total environmental impact?

During the initial stages of the design process, a designer may find a full Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) too complex and time-consuming to perform.

Which of the following is a characteristic of using an environmental impact assessment matrix as an alternative tool during this phase?

options.content: It provides a highly detailed quantitative analysis of all energy inputs.
It allows for a rapid, qualitative evaluation across all stages of the product life cycle.
It focuses exclusively on the environmental impacts of raw material extraction.
It is a legally mandated document required for the sale of all consumer electronics.
